bugs.error_message: Wrong reference: fmtDate should be formatDateTimeCsv
User: 'the time captured in the invoices is incorrect it could be because it's not adding +3 for bahrain time zone'
bugs.root_cause: Display-only bug; storage was already correct. Verified: Postgres TimeZone=GMT, receipts.created_at is 'timestamp without time zone', and JS now() vs PG now() drift measured 0.8s — so timestamps are stored in clean UTC. The pages (contacts/receipts/raffle) are SERVER components, so Date.toLocaleString() with no explicit zone formats in the SERVER's timezone, which is UTC on Vercel. Every invoice therefore rendered 3 hours early in production. It looked correct in local dev ONLY because the dev machine's zone is Asia/Bahrain, so the bug could never reproduce locally. The CSV export shared the fault via toISOString() (UTC by definition), and the export filename used toISOString().slice(0,10), which stamps yesterday's date between 00:00-03:00 Bahrain.
bugs.fix: Added lib/datetime.ts exposing formatDateTime / formatDateTimeCsv / todayInBahrain, all passing timeZone:'Asia/Bahrain' explicitly to module-level Intl.DateTimeFormat instances (hourCycle:'h23' so midnight renders 00:00, not 24:00). Replaced every toLocaleString()/toISOString() in app/contacts/page.tsx, app/receipts/page.tsx, app/raffle/page.tsx and app/api/export/route.ts; deleted the now-redundant fmtDate helper. CSV headers relabelled 'Timestamp (Bahrain)' / 'Last Seen (Bahrain)' since cells are no longer ISO/UTC. No data migration required. Verified by running the real formatter against real DB rows under TZ=UTC: IN-000545 went from '7/15/2026, 11:35:11 AM' to '15/07/2026, 14:35'. 9 unit tests in lib/datetime.test.ts pass identically under TZ=UTC, TZ=Asia/Bahrain and TZ=America/New_York.

bugs.error_message: Security audit finding #2: sign-in had no brute-force protection — unlimited bcrypt.compare attempts per second, no lockout, no rate limit, and no log that an attack was happening.
bugs.root_cause: authorize() in auth.ts looked up the user and compared the password with no attempt counter of any kind. A public portal + password auth + no lockout = credential stuffing works and leaves no trace. Two related holes in the same function: an unknown email returned before bcrypt ran, so response time (~900ms vs instant) enumerated the staff list (audit finding #9); and the bcrypt cost 10 was copy-pasted at three call sites (auth.ts, admin/users/actions.ts, prisma/seed.ts) so it could drift.
bugs.fix: Added login_attempts table (migration 0003) counting failures per email and per IP. lib/login-throttle.ts holds the pure policy (email: 5 failures → lock, doubling 1→2→4→8→15 min cap; IP: 30 failures, deliberately loose because a shop's cashiers share one NAT and locking the store mid-shift stops them scanning). lib/login-attempts.ts holds the Postgres reads/writes plus opportunistic GC. auth.ts throws a CredentialsSignin subclass whose code carries the wait ('too_many_attempts:60'), which the sign-in form renders as a human message. Enumeration closed via lib/password.ts ABSENT_USER_HASH — a bcrypt hash of an unrecorded random value, compared against when the email is unknown so the timing matches. BCRYPT_COST now defined once and shared. Failed sign-ins are console.warn'd.

bugs.error_message: Security audit finding #3: the webhook failed OPEN — if no app secret could be resolved, signature verification silently switched off and /api/webhook accepted anything from anyone.
bugs.root_cause: lib/webhook.ts verifySignatureAgainstAny() began `if (appSecrets.length === 0) return true` — documented and unit-tested as 'verification off'. getAppSecrets() built that list from META_APP_SECRET plus each store's decrypted secret, and a decrypt failure only logged and continued. So a wrong/rotated ENCRYPTION_KEY, or a restored DB, emptied the list and turned verification off — precisely when it mattered most. The insecure state was indistinguishable from the secure one in the logs.
bugs.fix: Removed the fail-open entirely; there is no 'no secrets = allow' path and no insecure escape hatch. The route now resolves candidate secrets for the phone_number_id the payload names: none resolvable -> 503 (our misconfiguration, so Meta retries and the events can still land once Settings/ENCRYPTION_KEY are fixed); signature mismatch -> 403. Decrypt failure logs loudly and yields no candidate rather than skipping verification. Every rejection names what to fix.

bugs.error_message: Security audit finding #4: one store could forge the other store's WhatsApp delivery statuses. The two stores are legally separate companies sharing one webhook URL.
bugs.root_cause: verifySignatureAgainstAny() returned a BOOLEAN — it discarded which secret matched, which is exactly the fact needed to scope the events. A payload signed by ANY store's app was accepted, then applyStatusEvent() looked the receipt up by wamid ALONE (db.receipt.findFirst({ where: { wamid } })) with no store scoping. Each store's manager sets their own app secret in Settings, so Morslon could sign a body carrying Modern Sources' wamids and rewrite their receipts.
bugs.fix: Replaced verifySignatureAgainstAny with findSigner(), which returns the matching candidate rather than a boolean. The route resolves the store from metadata.phone_number_id (safe to read unverified: it only picks WHICH secret to check, and a lie fails verification), verifies against that store's secret only, then applies two layers: (1) any change whose phone_number_id != the signer's is refused as cross-store, blocking a mixed body signed by one app; (2) the receipt lookup is scoped { wamid, storeId: signer.store.id }, so a wamid alone is not proof of ownership. logIncomingMessage now uses the signing store instead of a second phone lookup. CAVEAT: while two stores share the META_APP_SECRET env fallback they can still sign for each other — per-store secrets in Settings are what make the isolation real.
bugs.fix_verified: Sent a real morslon-signed body smuggling a change for another phone_number_id carrying receipt IN-00087's real wamid marked 'failed' (failed always wins over 'read', so it would have been visible). IN-00087 stayed 'read' with no error. Also confirmed on real data that the same wamid resolves under morslon but is not found under modern-sources.

bugs.error_message: RACE_CONDITION_HANDLING.md as written would have shipped an advisory lock that protected nothing: it specified pg_advisory_xact_lock() but left the transaction at Serializable.
bugs.root_cause: Under SERIALIZABLE (and REPEATABLE READ) Postgres freezes the transaction snapshot at the FIRST statement — which, with this pattern, is the lock acquisition itself. The snapshot is taken before the function blocks. So a scan that queues on the lock acquires it and then still reads the world as it was BEFORE the winner committed: MAX(entry_number) comes back stale and it allocates a duplicate entry number anyway. The lock appears to work while doing nothing. READ COMMITTED takes a fresh snapshot per statement, so reads after the lock is granted see the previous scan's committed rows.
bugs.fix: Switched the scan transaction to Prisma.TransactionIsolationLevel.ReadCommitted and took the advisory lock as the first statement inside it. Proved empirically: two txns contending for the same key, A inserts+commits while B waits — under Serializable B counted 0 after acquiring the lock (1 was committed); under ReadCommitted B counted 1.

bugs.error_message: Raw query failed. Code: `N/A`. Message: `Failed to deserialize column of type 'void'` / DriverAdapterError: UnsupportedNativeDataType { kind: 'UnsupportedNativeDataType', type: 'void' } — every scan failed with P2010.
bugs.root_cause: Took the advisory lock with tx.$queryRaw`SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(...)`. pg_advisory_xact_lock() returns void, and $queryRaw tries to deserialize the result columns; the @prisma/adapter-neon driver cannot map Postgres 'void'. RACE_CONDITION_HANDLING.md actually had this right with $executeRaw — I 'corrected' it to $queryRaw on the reasoning that SELECT belongs in $queryRaw, and broke every scan.
bugs.fix: Use tx.$executeRaw for the lock: it only reports a row count and never deserializes columns, so the void return is fine. Caught immediately by the concurrency test (0/5 scans succeeded) — it would have taken down every scan in production.

bugs.error_message: A raffle entry-number collision returned HTTP 500 "Something went wrong while logging the scan" and lost the scan, instead of retrying.
bugs.root_cause: The P2002 handler only mapped the violation to a clean error when err.meta.target included 'invoice'; anything else — realistically the (store_id, entry_number) unique index — fell through to `throw err` and surfaced as a 500. Only P2034 was retried.
bugs.fix: Retry non-invoice P2002 within maxAttempts (re-reads MAX(entry_number) and takes the next free number). Also stopped retrying ScanError itself — a genuine duplicate invoice was being re-attempted three times — and mapped statement_timeout (SQLSTATE 57014) to a friendly ScanError('System busy, please try again', 'busy') -> HTTP 503 rather than a raw 500.

bugs.error_message: User: 'is the connector to google spreadsheet work?' — Settings showed a 'Sheet ID (optional)' field that saved fine and did absolutely nothing.
bugs.root_cause: The Google Sheets integration was never built, but the UI implied it was. stores.google_sheet_id was written by the settings form and never read by anything; no google library was in package.json; GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_* appeared only in .env.example and were read nowhere. A manager could paste a real sheet ID, get 'Settings saved', and believe their data was being backed up when nothing was ever written.
bugs.fix: Built the weekly backup for real: lib/google-sheets.ts (service-account JWT signed with node:crypto + Sheets REST, no googleapis SDK — it is huge and would tax every cold start), lib/backup.ts (row building + never-shrink guard), app/api/cron/backup/route.ts (CRON_SECRET-authenticated, per-store, one store's failure cannot stop the other), vercel.json weekly schedule, /api/cron made public in auth.config.ts. Relabelled the field 'Weekly backup (Google Sheets)' with a hint explaining what it does and that the sheet must be shared with the service account.
Changed href: '/admin/users', label: 'Users', roles: ['admin'] → '/admin/sheets', label: 'Sheets', roles: ['admin']

bugs.error_message: Google Sheets sync failed for every store: 'error:1E08010C:DECODER routines::unsupported'. User had set up the service account and redeployed, but nothing worked.
bugs.root_cause: GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_PRIVATE_KEY held the service account JSON's `private_key_id` (a 40-char hex string) instead of its `private_key` (a ~1700-char PEM). Both fields sit next to each other in the downloaded JSON and the names are nearly identical. node:crypto's createSign could not parse it, so the JWT was never built and Google was never contacted. The same wrong value was in both .env.local and Vercel.
bugs.fix: Diagnosed by checking the value's shape rather than its contents: length 40, no -----BEGIN prefix, matches /^[0-9a-f]{40}$/ = private_key_id. User re-pasted the real private_key (1732 chars, signs fine). NOTE FOR FUTURE: a read-only probe (buildServiceAccountJwt + tabRowCount on each tab) verifies key + sharing + sheet id + tab names WITHOUT writing anything — always do that before running a sync that overwrites.

bugs.root_cause: hover: bg-amber-50 disabled:opacity-40 dark:border-amber-700 dark:text-amber-400 dark:hover:bg-amber-950/40" → bg-black/[.04] disabled:opacity-40 dark:border-white/15 dark:hover:bg-white/[.06]"
bugs.fix: Changed hover: bg-amber-50 disabled:opacity-40 dark:border-amber-700 dark:text-amber-400 dark:hover:bg-amber-950/40" → bg-black/[.04] disabled:opacity-40 dark:border-white/15 dark:hover:bg-white/[.06]"

bugs.error_message: User: 'let's add a button to sync one store. maybe we don't want to sync both' — the admin Sheets page only offered all-or-nothing syncs.
bugs.root_cause: The page shipped with only global 'Sync now' / 'Sync now (force)' buttons, so an admin could not refresh one company's sheet without also rewriting the other's. lib/sheets-sync.ts already exported a per-store syncStore(); nothing exposed it.
bugs.fix: Added syncOneStore(formData) server action (validates storeId against the DB, requireAdmin) and per-store 'Sync this store' + 'Force' buttons on each card. Buttons are disabled when the store has no Sheet ID or credentials are missing, rather than reporting 'skipped'. Global button reduced to 'Sync all stores' with NO force option — forcing lets a sheet shrink, so it should be decided one store at a time rather than applied to both companies in one click. Verified only the chosen store's sheet and last_sync_at change.

bugs.error_message: Sessions never meaningfully expired — a signed-in till stayed signed in for the NextAuth default 30 days (flagged in the security audit as a lower-severity finding).
bugs.root_cause: session was `{ strategy: 'jwt' }` with no maxAge, so the default 30-day token applied. A shared till left unattended, or a device taken home, stayed authenticated indefinitely. Firing someone did not end their session either — only deleting the profile did, via getCurrentUserProfile() returning null.
bugs.fix: session.maxAge = 2h with updateAge = 5min. maxAge alone would NOT slide: NextAuth only rewrites the token once updateAge has elapsed, so without it an active cashier would be logged out mid-shift at the 2h mark. Verified empirically: the issued cookie expires in exactly 2.00h (note: NextAuth sets `Expires`, NOT `Max-Age` — do not grep for Max-Age), and after 5m20s of activity the expiry moved forward 321s with the session still valid.

bugs.error_message: User: 'lets use user name instead of emails' — staff had to sign in with an email address they mostly do not have.
bugs.root_cause: UserProfile had a required unique email used as the login credential. Retail till staff rarely have a work email, so accounts were being given synthetic addresses (cashier1@morslon.com) that nobody could remember or type quickly on a shared device.
bugs.fix: Migration 0005: added unique `username`, backfilled from the email local-part (admin@morslon.com -> admin), made `email` nullable, renamed receipts.cashier_email -> cashier_username. Order matters: add the column NULLABLE, backfill, THEN set NOT NULL — adding NOT NULL to a populated table fails outright. Postgres allows many NULLs under a unique index, so email stays unique without blocking accounts that have none. Switched authorize() to look up by username, renamed the throttle key prefix email: -> user: (ACCOUNT_KEY_PREFIX, shared so login-attempts.ts cannot drift), updated the sign-in form, admin create/edit/search, nav, receipts column, CSV header and the seed script (SEED_ADMIN_USERNAME). Usernames are normalized lowercase and validated /^[a-z0-9._-]{3,32}$/.

bugs.error_message: CSV export silently truncated at 10,000 rows. The prize draw is run off exported data, so entries beyond 10k could never win and nothing said so.
bugs.root_cause: MAX_ROWS = 10000 was a hard `take:` on every export query, with all rows buffered in memory and joined into one string. At 50k raffle entries the file contained 10k rows and no warning; at 300k it dropped 290k. Modelled from real row shapes, not guessed.
bugs.fix: Removed the cap entirely and switched to a ReadableStream that pages the DB (PAGE_SIZE 5000) while the browser downloads, so memory is flat regardless of size. Paging is by id CURSOR, not skip/offset, which degrades badly past ~100k. Every orderBy now ends with `{ id: 'asc' }` so the ordering is total — rows sharing a timestamp cannot shuffle between pages and get duplicated or skipped. A mid-stream failure appends an 'EXPORT FAILED — this file is incomplete' row, because the 200 status is already sent and a short file would otherwise look complete. Verified end-to-end: 25,000 entries exported in 4.7s, entry 1 through 25,000, no gaps.

bugs.error_message: The nightly Google Sheets sync would have started failing partway through the campaign: it wrote each tab in ONE request, which Google rejects or times out on past roughly 50k rows.
bugs.root_cause: overwriteTab() PUT the entire values array in a single request. Measured payload sizes from real row shapes: 50k entries = 4.3 MB, 100k = 8.6 MB, 300k = 25.9 MB. The user confirmed the PRIZE DRAW IS RUN OFF THE SHEET, so a failed or stale sync means entries missing from the draw — this was draw integrity, not just a performance issue. (Google's 10M cell limit is further out, ~2M cells at 300k entries, so request size bites first.)
bugs.fix: Write in ROWS_PER_REQUEST = 10,000 chunks at computed A1 offsets (A1, A10001, A20001...). Batches go top-down and the large tabs are append-only in a stable order (raffle by entryNumber, log by createdAt), so a failure partway leaves earlier rows correct and later ones stale — never scrambled — and the next sync repairs it. The trim of leftover rows still runs afterwards. Unit-tested with a stubbed fetch: 300k rows becomes exactly 30 requests, none over 10k, every row written once, offsets contiguous.

bugs.error_message: User: 'add dark/light theme mode' — the app followed the OS via prefers-color-scheme with no way to override it.
bugs.root_cause: Dark mode existed but was entirely OS-driven: globals.css used @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) and every component uses Tailwind `dark:` utilities, which in Tailwind v4 also follow prefers-color-scheme by default. A toggle that only swapped the CSS variables would have left every `dark:border-white/10` in the app still obeying the OS — the two would visibly disagree.
bugs.fix: Drive the theme from a `data-theme` attribute on <html> and redirect Tailwind's variant at it with `@custom-variant dark (&:where([data-theme="dark"], [data-theme="dark"] *))`. localStorage holds the PREFERENCE (light|dark|system); data-theme holds the RESOLVED value and is always concrete, so the CSS never reasons about 'system'. A blocking inline <script> in <head> (components/theme-script.tsx) applies it before first paint — anything waiting on React would paint the wrong theme first. <html suppressHydrationWarning> because the script legitimately changes the attribute before hydration; the toggle starts as 'system' on both server and client and reads localStorage in an effect, so no marker flashes as active. matchMedia listener keeps 'system' live without a reload. Toggle added to the nav AND the sign-in page, which has no nav.
bugs.fix_verified: In-browser with computed styles: OS prefers dark, clicked Light -> data-theme=light, body #fff, and a `dark:border-white/15` input border flipped to black/0.1, proving the Tailwind variant follows the attribute not the OS. Survived reload (script in <head>). System restored OS-following. No hydration warnings in console.
